title: BENCHMARKING MACHINE LEARNING ROBUSTNESS IN COVID-19 SPIKE SEQUENCE CLASSIFICATION [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## BENCHMARKING MACHINE LEARNING ROBUSTNESS IN COVID-19 SPIKE SEQUENCE CLASSIFICATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

The rapid spread of the COVID-19 pandemic has resulted in an unprecedented amount of sequence data of the SARS-CoV-2 viral genome — millions of sequences and counting. This amount of data, while being orders of magnitude beyond the capacity of traditional approaches to understanding the diversity, dynamics and evolution of viruses, is nonetheless a rich resource for machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) approaches as alternatives for extracting such important information from these data. It is of hence utmost importance to design a framework for testing and benchmarking the robustness of these ML and DL approaches.

This paper the first (to our knowledge) to explore such a framework. In this paper, we introduce several ways to perturb SARS-CoV-2 spike protein sequences in ways that mimic the error profiles of common sequencing platforms such as Illumina and PacBio. We show from experiments on a wide array of ML approaches from naive Bayes to logistic regression, that DL approaches are more robust (and accurate) to such adverarial attacks to the input sequences, while _k_ -mer based feature vector representations are more robust than the baseline one-hot embedding. Our benchmarking framework may developers of futher ML and DL techniques to properly assess their approaches towards understanding the behaviour of the SARS-CoV-2 virus, or towards avoiding possible future pandemics.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

A novel (RNA) coronavirus was identified in January 2020, which began the COVID-19 pandemic that is still ongoing today. With the help of sequencing technology and phylogenetic analysis, the scientific community disclosed that this novel coronavirus has 50% similarity with the Middle-Eastern Repiratory Syndrome Coronavirus (MERS-CoV), 79% sequencing similarity to Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coronavirus (SARS-CoV) — also known simply as “SARS” — and more than 85% similarity with coronavirusus found in bats. Further studies confirmed that bats are the likely reservoir of these coronaviruses; however, the ecological separation of bats from humans indicates that some other organisms may have acted as intermediate hosts. Considering all scientific evidence, the International Committee on Taxonomy of Viruses named the novel RNA virus SARS-CoV-2 (Wu et al., 2020; Park, 2020; Zhang & Holmes, 2020).

RNA viruses generally introduce errors during replication, the resulting mutations incorporated into the viral genome after repeated replication within a single host, generating a heterogenous population of viral quasispecies. However, SARS-CoV-2 has an excellent proofreading mechanism that encodes a nonstructural protein 14 (nsp14) allowing it to have a 10-fold lower mutation rate than the typical RNA viruses. Epidemiologists estimate that SARS-CoV-2 has 33 genomic mutations per year on average. Some of these mutations are advantageous, leading to the more infections variants of SARS-CoV-2 that continue to emerge (Nelson, 2021). Because of this relatively slow process of accumlating mutations, and the fact that each major variant can be characterized or differentiated by a handfull of mutations (SARS-CoV-2 Variant Classifications and Definitions, 2021), small perturbations (errors) in the sequence should not lead to mistaking one variant from another. Moreover, most of these changes occur in the S gene — the segment of the genome which encodes the surface, or spike, protein (see Figure 1) — hence, characterizing variants using (the resulting tran-

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

scribed) spike proteins is sufficient for the classification task (SARS-CoV-2 Variant Classifications and Definitions, 2021; Kuzmin et al., 2020).

Figure 1: The SARS-CoV-2 genome codes for several proteins, including the surface, or spike protein. Sequence of spike protein is important to understand various molecular mechanisms of SARS-Cov-2. While incorporation of error in spike sequences can bias the final result (Huang et al., 2020).

The diminishing cost of next-generation sequencing (NGS) technology has aided scientists from different parts of the world to generate SARS-CoV-2 whole-genome sequencing (WGS). The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) of the United States has also provided a wealth of information on resources, tools, and protocols for SARS-CoV-2 WGS from different sequencing platforms such as Illumina, PacBio, and Ion Torrent. Finally, the Global Initiative on Sharing All Influenza Data (GISAID) hosts the largest SARS-CoV-2 genome sequencing dataset to date — the largest of any virus in history, with millions of sequences. This unprecedented amount of genomic data generation and easy availability allowed researchers to explore the molecular mechanism, genetic variability, evolutionary progress, and capability of development and spread of novel variants of the virus. On the other hand, this amount of data exceeds the capacity of methods such as Nextstrain (Hadfield et al., 2018) or even the more recent IQTREE2 (Minh et al., 2020) by several orders of magnitude — a Big Data challenge. As a result, recent alternative approaches based on clustering and classification of sequences, _e.g._ , to identify major variants, have appeared in the literature (Melnyk et al., 2020; Ali et al., 2021a;b; Ali & Patterson, 2021), with promising accuracy and scalability properties.

Many issues still remain, however, such as sequencing errors being mistaken for mutations in different analyses, when studying the evolutionary and transmission pattern of the SARS-CoV-2 (GISAID History, 2021; Arons et al., 2020), or other viruses. Incorporation of error in NGS sequences due to contamination in sample preparation, sequencing technology, or genome assembly methodology are other confounding factors. Generally, computational biologists filter those sequences having errors or mask those sequence fragments having errors. For example, each GISAID (GISAID Website, 2021) sequence is a consensus sequence from the intra-host viral population sampled from the patient, averaging out the minor variations which exist in this population. While such a consensus sequence is a good representative of this population, _i.e._ , it is still precise enough to capture the SARS-CoV-2 variant harboured by the infected individual, it comes at the cost of losing this important information, such as these minor variations. Such minor variations, when given enough time to evolve, _e.g._ , within an immunocompromized individual, can become dominant — one of the theories theory behind the emergence of the Alpha variant (Frampton et al., 2021).

What this means is that many of machine learning approaches towards clustering and classification of sequences (Ali et al., 2021a;b; Ali & Patterson, 2021) have been operating under rather idealized conditions of virtually error-free consensus sequences. Moveover, these methods rely on a _k_ -mer based feature vector representation — an approach that does not even rely on alignment of the sequences, something which can also introduce bias (Golubchik et al., 2007). Such a framework should easily cope with errors as well — something machine learning approaches can do very

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

naturally (Du et al., 2021). There is hence a great need for some way to reliably benchmark such methods for robustness to errors, which is what we carry out in this paper.

We highlight the main contributions of this paper as follows:

- We propose several ways of introducing errors into spike sequences which reflect the error profiles of modern NGS technologies such as Illumina and PacBio;
- We demonstrate that the _k_ -mer based approach is more robust to such errors when compared to the baseline (one-hot encoding); and
- We show that deep learning is generally more robust in handling these errors than machine learning models.

Moreover, we extend our error testing procedure as a framework for bechmarking the performance of different ML methods in terms of classification accuracy and robustness to different types of simulated random errors in the sequences. The two types of errors that we introduce are “consecutive” and “random” errors (see Sec. 3.4). Random errors are just point mutations, which happen uniformly at random along the protein sequence, simulating closely the behaviour of Illumina sequenceing technolgies (Stoler & Nekrutenko, 2021). Consecutive errors, on the other hand, are small subsequences of consective errors, which can model insertion-delection (indel) errors which are common in third generation long-reads technologies such as Pacific Biosciences (PacBio) SMRT sequencing (Dohm et al., 2020).

This paper is structured as follows. In Sec. 2 we discuss related work. In Sec. 3 we discuss some approaches we benchmark, and then how we benchmark: the type of adversarial attacks we use. Sce. 4 details the experiments, and Sec. 5 gives the results. Finally, we conclude this paper in Sec. 6.

## 2 RELATED WORK

Assessing and benchmarking the robustness of ML or DL approaches by a series of adversarial attacks is popular in the image classification domain (Hendrycks & Dietterich, 2019), but there are others that are closer to the domain of molecular data. In (Schwalbe-Koda et al., 2021), the authors provide a series of realistic adversarial attacks to benchmark methods which predict chemical properties from atomistic simulations _e.g._ , molecular conformation, reactions and phase transitions. Even closer to the subject of our paper — protein sequences – the authors of (Jha et al., 2021) show that methods, such as AlphaFold (Jumper et al., 2021) and RoseTTAFold (Baek et al., 2021) which employ deep neural networks to predict protein conformation are not robust: producing drastically different protein structures as a result of very small biologically meaningful perturbations in the protein sequence. Our approach is similar, albeit with the different goal of classification: namely, to explore how a small number of point mutations (simulating the error introduced certain types of NGS technologies) can affect the downstream classification of different machine learning and deep learning approaches. After getting the numerical representation, a popular approach is to get the kernel matrix and give that matrix as input to traditional machine learning classifiers like support vector machines (SVM) (Leslie et al., 2003; Farhan et al., 2017; Kuksa et al., 2012). However, these methods are expensive in terms of space complexity. Authors in (Ali & Patterson, 2021; Kuzmin et al., 2020) proposes an efficient embedding method for classification and clustering of spike sequences. However, their approaches are either not scalable or perform poorly on bigger datasets.

## 3 PROPOSED APPROACH

In this section, we start by explaining the baseline model for spike sequence classification. After that, we will explain our deep learning model in detail.

## 3.1 ONE-HOT ENCODING (OHE) BASED EMBEDDING

Authors in (Kuzmin et al., 2020) propose that classification of viral hosts of the coronavirus can be done by using spike sequences only. For this purpose, a fixed-length one-hot encoding based feature vector is generated for the spike sequences. In spike sequence, we have 21 unique characters (amino acids) that are _“ACDEFGHIKLMNPQRSTVWXY”_ . Also, note that the length of each spike

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

sequence is 1273 with the stopping character ‘*’ at the 1274 _[th]_ position. When we design the OHE based numerical vector for the spike sequence, the length of numerical vector will be 21 _×_ 1273 = 26733. This high dimensionality could create the problem of “Curse of Dimensionality (CoD)”. To solve CoD problem, any dimensionality reduction method can be used such as Principal Component Analysis (Abdi & Williams, 2010). After reducing the dimensions of the feature vectors, classical Machine Learning (ML) algorithms can be applied to classify the spike sequences. One major problem with such OHE based representation is that it does not preserve the order of the amino acids very efficiently (Ali et al., 2021a). If we compute the pair-wise euclidean distance between any two OHE based vectors, the overall distance will not be effected if a random pair of amino acids are swapped for those two feature vectors. Since the order of amino acids is important in case of sequential data, OHE fails to give us efficient results (Ali et al., 2021a). In this paper, we use OHE as baseline embedding method.

## 3.2 K-MERS BASED REPRESENTATION

A popular approach to preserve the ordering of the sequential information is to take the sliding window based substrings (called mers) of length _k_ . This _k_ -mers based representation is recently proven to be useful in classifying the spike sequences effectively (Ali et al., 2021a) (see Figure 2 for example of k-mers). In this approach, first, the k-mers of length _k_ are computed for each spike

Figure 2: Example of different length _k_ -mers in a spike sequence “MDPEG”.

sequence. Then a fixed length frequency vector is generated corresponding to each spike sequence, which contains the count of each k-mer in that sequence. One advantage of using k-mers based approach is that it is an _“alignment free”_ method unlike OHE, which requires the sequences to be aligned to the reference genome.

**Remark 1** _Sequence alignment is an expensive process and requires reference sequence (genome) (Chowdhury & Garai, 2017; Denti et al., 2021). It may also introduce bias into the result (Golubchik et al., 2007)._

The total number of k-mers in a given spike sequences are:

where _N_ is the length of the sequence. The variable _k_ is the user defined parameter. In this paper, we take _k_ = 3 (decided empirically). Since we have 1273 length spike sequences, the total number of k-mers that we can have for any spike sequence is 1273 _−_ 3 + 1 = 1271.

## 3.2.1 FREQUENCY VECTOR GENERATION

After generating the k-mers, the next step is to generate the fixed-length numerical representation (frequency vector) for the set of k-mers in a spike sequence. Let the set of amino acids in the whole dataset is represented by alphabet Σ. Now, length of the frequency vector will be _|_ Σ _|[k]_ (all possible combinations of k-mers in Σ of length _k_ ). Recall that in our dataset, we have 21 unique amino acids in any spike sequence. Therefore, the length of frequency vector in our case would be 21[3] = 9261 (when we take _k_ = 3).

4

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Note that CoD could be a problem in case of k-mers based numerical representation of the spike sequence. To deal with this problem, authors in (Ali & Patterson, 2021) use an approximate kernel method that map such vectors into low dimensional euclidean space using an approach, called Random Fourier Features (RFF) (Rahimi et al., 2007). Unlike kernel trick, which compute the inner product between the lifted data points _φ_ (i.e. _⟨φ_ ( _a_ ) _, φ_ ( _b_ ) _⟩_ = _f_ ( _a, b_ ), where _a, b ∈R[d]_ and f(a,b) is any positive definite function), RFF maps the data into low dimensional euclidean inner product space. More formally:

RFF tries to approximate the inner product between any pair of transformed points.

where _z_ is low dimensional representation. Since _z_ is the approximate representation, we can use it as an input for the classical ML models and analyse their behavior (as done in Spike2Vec (Ali & Patterson, 2021)). However, we show that such approach performs poorly on larger size datasets (hence poor scalability).

## 3.3 KERAS CLASSIFIER

We use a deep learning-based model called the Keras Classification model (also called Keras classifier) to further improve the performance that we got from Spike2Vec. For keras classifier, we use a sequential constructor. It contains a fully connected network with one hidden layer that contains neurons equals to the length of the feature vector (i.e. 9261). We use ”rectifier” activation function for this classifier. Moreover, we use “softmax” activation function in the output layer. At last, we use the efficient Adam gradient descent optimization algorithm with “sparse categorical crossentropy” loss function (used for multi-class classification problem). It computes the crossentropy loss between the labels and predictions. The batch size and number of epochs are taken as 100 and 10, respectively for training the model. For the input to this keras classifier, we separately use OHE and _k_ -mers based frequency vectors.
**Remark 2** _Note that we are using “sparse categorical crossentropy” rather than simple “categorical crossentropy” because we are using integer labels rather than one-hot representation of labels._

## 3.4 ADVERSARIAL EXAMPLES CREATION

We use two types of approaches to generate adversarial examples so that we can test the robustness of our proposed model. These approaches are “Random Error generation” and “Consecutive Error generation”.

In random error generation, we randomly select a fraction of spike sequences (we call them the set of errored sequences for reference) from the test set (i.e. 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%). For each of the spike sequence in the set of errored sequences, we randomly select a fraction of amino acids (i.e. 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%) and flip their value randomly. At the end, we replace these errored sequences set with the corresponding set of original spike sequences in the test set. The ideas is that this simulates the errors made by NGS technologies such as Illumina (Stoler & Nekrutenko, 2021).

In consecutive error generation, the first step is the same as in random error generation (getting random set of spike sequences from the test set “set of errored sequences”). For this set of errored sequences, rather than randomly flipping a specific percentage of amino acid’s values for each spike sequence (i.e. 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%), we flip the values for the same fraction of amino acids but those amino acids are consecutive and at random position in the spike sequence. More formally, it is a consecutive set of amino acids (at random position) in the spike sequence for which we flip the values. At the end, we replace these errored sequences set with the corresponding set of original spike sequences in the test set. The idea is that this simulates indel errors, which are frequencly found in third generation long-read technologies such as PacBio (Dohm et al., 2020).

Using the two approaches to generate adversarial examples, we generate a new test set and evaluate the performance of the ML and deep learning models. To measure the performance, we also apply two different strategies. One strategy is called Accuracy and the other is called robustness. In the case of the Accuracy, we compute the average accuracy, precision, recall, F1 (weighted), F1 (Macro), and ROC-AUC for the whole test set including adversarial and non-adversarial examples.

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

For our second strategy (robustness), we only consider the adversarial examples (set of errored spike sequences) rather than considering the whole test set and compute average accuracy, precision, recall, F1 (weighted), F1 (Macro), and ROC-AUC for them.

## 4 EXPERIMENTAL SETUP

All experiments are conducted using an Intel(R) Xeon(R) CPU E7-4850 v4 @ 2 _._ 10GHz having Ubuntu 64 bit OS (16 _._ 04 _._ 7 LTS Xenial Xerus) with 3023 GB memory. Our pre-processed data is also available online[1] , which can be used after agreeing to terms and conditions of GISAID[2] . For the classification algorithms, we use 1% data for training and 99% for testing. Note that our data split and pre-processing follow those of (Ali & Patterson, 2021).

## 4.1 DATASET STATISTICS

We used the (aligned) spike protein from a popular and publicly available database of SARS-CoV-2 sequences, GISAID. In our dataset, we have 2 _,_ 519 _,_ 386 spike sequences along with the COVID-19 variant information. The total number of unique variants in our dataset are 1327. Since not all variants have significant representation in our data, we only select the variants having more than 10 _,_ 000 sequences. After this preprocessing, we are left with 1 _,_ 995 _,_ 195 spike sequences. The dataset statistics for the prepossessed data are given in Table 1. Since most of the variants are new, we do not have all the information available for all them. Therefore, we put “-” in the field in Table 1 for which we do not have any information available online.

Table 1: Dataset Statistics for different variants in our data. The S/Gen. column represents number of mutations on the Spike (S) gene / entire genome. The total number of spike sequences (and corresponding variants) 1 _,_ 995 _,_ 195 after preprocessing (Ali & Patterson, 2021)

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

To visualise if there is any (natural) clustering in our data, we generated 2d representation of the k-mers based frequency vectors using t-distributed stochastic neighbor embedding (t-SNE) approach (Van der M. & Hinton, 2008). The t-SNE plot for some of the popular variants is given in Figure 3. Note that we have not given the t-SNE plot for all variants. This is because t-SNE method is computationally very expensive (runtime is _O_ ( _N_[2] ), where _N_ is the number of data-points (Pezzotti et al., 2016)) and take a lot of time on _≈_ 1 _._ 9 million sequences.

Figure 3: t-SNE plot for the k-mers based feature vectors of spike sequences.

## 5 RESULTS AND DISCUSSION

In this section, we first show the comparison of our deep learning model with the baselines. We then show the results for the two approaches for adversarial examples generation and compare different ML and DL methods. Overall, we elucidate our key findings in the following subsections.

## 5.1 EFFECTIVENESS OF DEEP LEARNING

Table 2 contains the (accuracy) results for our keras classifier and its comparison with different ML models namely Naive Nayes (NB), Logistic Regression (LR), and Ridge Classifier (RC). For keras classifier, we use both OHE and k-mers based embedding approaches separately. We can observe from the results that keras classifier with k-mers based frequency vectors is by far the best approach as compared to the other baselines.
Table 2: Variants Classification Results (1% training set and 99% testing set) for top 22 variants (1995195 spike sequences).

To test the robustness of these ML and DL methods, we use both “consecutive error generation” and “random error generation” separately. Table 3 shows the (accuracy) results (using keras classifier with k-mers because that was the best model from Table 2) for the consecutive error generation

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

method (using different fraction of spike sequences from the test set and different fraction of amino acids flips in each spike sequence). We can observe that keras classifier is able to perform efficiently even with higher proportion of error.
Table 3: Accuracy results for the whole test set (consecutive error seq.) for Keras Classifier with k-mers and different % of errors.

The robustness results for the consecutive error generation method are given in Table 4. Although we cannot see any clear pattern in this case, the keras classifier is giving us comparatively higher performance in some of the settings.
Table 4: Robustness (only error seq. in test set) results (consecutive error seq.) for Keras Classifier with k-mers and different % of errors.

Table 5 (in appendix) contains the accuracy results for the keras classifier (with k-mers based frequency vectors as input) with random errored sequences approach. We can observe that our DL model is able to maintain higher accuracy even with 20% of the spike sequences having some fraction of error in the test set. Similarly, the robustness results are given in Table 6 (in Appendix).

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

To visually compare the accuracy of the average accuracy for the consecutive errored sequences approach, we plot the average accuracies in Figure 4a. Similarly, Figure 5 (in Appendix) contains the robustness results for the same two approaches.

**----- Start of picture text -----**

(b)
(a)
**----- End of picture text -----**

Figure 4: (a) Accuracy Comparison of the Consecutive error generation and Random error generation approaches for different fraction of adversarial spike sequences (note that these results are for one run). (b) Accuracy (x axis) vs Robustness (y axis) plot (for average accuracy values) for different ML and DL methods for 10% adversarial sequences from the test set with 10% amino acids flips.

The accuracy and the robustness results for the other ML models (using Spike2Vec approach) are given in Tables 7 and Table 8, respectively in the appendix. From the results, we can conclude that our deep learning based model is more accurate and robust than other compared machine learning models. Another interesting outcome from the results is that k-mers based feature vector is more robust that traditional one-hot embedding. This is a kind of “proof of concept” that since k-mers preserve the order of the amino acids in a spike sequence (as order matters in genomic sequence data), it outperforms the traditional OHE by a significant margin.

The accuracy vs robustness comparison (for average accuracy values) of different ML and DL methods for 10% adversarial sequences from the test set with 10% amino acids flips is given in Figure 4b. We can see that keras classifier performs best as compared to the other ML methods. This shows that not only our DL method show better predictive performance, but is also more robust as compared to the other ML models.

## 6 CONCLUSION

One interesting future extension is using other alignment free methods such as Minimizers, which have been successful in representing metagenomics data. Since an intra-host viral population can be viewed as a metagenomics sample, this could be appropriate in this context. Another future direction is introducing more adversarial attacks resembling, in more detail, the error profiles of specific sequencing technologies. One could even fine-tune this to the particular experimental setting in which one obtained a sample, similar to sequencing reads simulators such as PBSIM (for PacBio reads).

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper provides a timely exploration of machine learning robustness in the context of SARS-CoV-2 protein sequence classification, specifically simulating sequencing errors. While the motivation is strong and the empirical results suggest that k-mer based deep learning models outperform traditional baselines, the paper suffers from significant methodological underspecification regarding the 'Keras Classifier' and the 'Robustness' metric. Furthermore, the 'Robustness' results presented in Table 4 appear highly unstable or contain errors that are not adequately explained, potentially undermining the claim that the model is robust to high error rates.

comments.title: Unexplained Performance Collapse in Robustness Metrics
comments.quote: Table 4: Robustness (only error seq. in test set) results (consecutive error seq.) for Keras Classifier with k-mers and different % of errors.
comments.explanation: In Table 4, the accuracy for '5% of sequences' with '10% error' drops to 0.15, and for '15% of sequences' with '15% error' it drops to 0.06. Given that there are 22 classes, an accuracy of 0.06 is worse than random chance (~0.045). However, other rows in the same table (e.g., 5% sequences with 20% error) show a jump back up to 0.60. There is no biological or algorithmic justification provided for why a 15% error rate would yield near-zero accuracy while a 20% error rate would yield 60% accuracy. This suggests either an error in the experimental pipeline, a radical instability in the Keras model, or a mislabeled table.

comments.title: Underspecified Deep Learning Model Architecture
comments.quote: For keras classifier, we use a sequential constructor. It contains a fully connected network with one hidden layer that contains neurons equals to the length of the feature vector (i.e. 9261).
comments.explanation: The authors describe a single hidden layer with 9,261 neurons. However, for a classification task with ~2 million samples and 22 classes, crucial details such as dropout, weight initialization, or the presence of batch normalization are missing. More importantly, the 'One-Hot' input (Section 3.1) has a dimensionality of 26,733. It is unclear if the hidden layer size is adjusted to 26,733 for the OHE baseline or stays at 9,261; if the latter, the projection logic is missing. This makes the DL component difficult to reproduce exactly.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Robustness Evaluation Metric
comments.quote: For our second strategy (robustness), we only consider the adversarial examples (set of errored spike sequences) rather than considering the whole test set...
comments.explanation: Evaluating accuracy only on perturbed sequences is a valid metric, but the authors do not clarify if the model was retrained on errored data or if these results represent zero-shot inference on perturbed strings from a model trained on clean data. Standard robustness benchmarking (like Hendrycks & Dietterich, 2019, cited by the authors) usually distinguishes between these. Given the high training times in Table 3/4, it looks like a new model might be trained for every error % (which is 'Data Augmentation' performance, not 'Robustness' of a single model). If a new model is trained for every error rate, the comparison is less about the model's inherent robustness and more about the quality of the specific training run.

comments.title: Logical Inconsistency in Consecutive Error Simulation
comments.quote: The idea is that this simulates indel errors, which are frequencly found in third generation long-read technologies such as PacBio (Dohm et al., 2020).
comments.explanation: The authors define 'Consecutive Error generation' as selecting a block of amino acids and 'flipping their values'. In bioinformatics, 'flipping values' (substitutions) is fundamentally different from 'indels' (insertions/deletions). Indels cause frame-shifts in DNA or gaps in protein alignments. Simply changing 'A' to 'V' over 10 consecutive positions simulates a region of high mutation/sequencing noise, but it does not simulate the length-varying nature of Indels. Claiming this mimics PacBio indel profiles is slightly misleading to a genomicist.

comments.title: Contradiction between Text and Table on Class Count
comments.quote: The total number of unique variants in our dataset are 1327... we only select the variants having more than 10,000 sequences... Table 2: Variants Classification Results... for top 22 variants
comments.explanation: Table 1 lists 22 variants that meet the criteria. Table 2 says 'top 22 variants.' However, an accuracy of 0.30 for Naive Bayes (Table 2) is mentioned. If the problem is 22-class classification, the baseline is ~4.5%. This matches. However, the ROC-AUC values (~0.56-0.61) are extremely low for a supervised task with 2 million samples, barely better than random. For common lineages like Alpha and Delta, one would expect much higher separability. This raises questions about whether the feature extraction (3-mers) is actually discarding too much information or if the 'labels' are too overlapping.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a timely and relevant contribution by introducing a benchmarking framework for assessing the robustness of machine learning models in SARS-CoV-2 spike sequence classification under realistic sequencing error models. The motivation is strong, the experimental setup is large-scale, and the use of k-mer and one-hot embeddings with adversarial perturbations reflective of Illumina and PacBio error profiles is well-conceived. The results suggest that deep learning models, particularly with k-mer representations, are more robust and accurate — a finding that aligns with expectations in representation learning. However, several issues in numerical consistency, metric interpretation, and methodological clarity — especially in the robustness evaluation and training protocol — raise concerns about the validity and reproducibility of the central claims. These issues are primarily moderate in severity but collectively affect confidence in the conclusions.

comments.title: Incorrect k-mer frequency vector dimensionality
comments.quote: Therefore, the length of frequency vector in our case would be 21[3] = 9261 (when we take k = 3).
comments.explanation: At first glance, 21^3 = 9261 appears correct for the number of possible 3-mers over 21 amino acids. However, in practice, many k-mer based methods use a sliding window over the sequence and count only observed k-mers, but here the authors explicitly state they are creating a fixed-length frequency vector of size |Σ|^k, which should indeed be 21^3 = 9261. The formula is mathematically correct. Moreover, the Keras classifier in Section 3.3 uses 9261 neurons in the hidden layer, consistent with this. Thus, while the notation '21[3]' is non-standard (should be 21^3), it appears to be a typographic convention rather than an error. No actual inconsistency is found upon cross-checking with model architecture and usage.

comments.title: Inconsistent application of error models in robustness metric
comments.quote: For our second strategy (robustness), we only consider the adversarial examples (set of errored spike sequences) rather than considering the whole test set and compute average accuracy, precision, recall, F1 (weighted), F1 (Macro), and ROC-AUC for them.
comments.explanation: The authors define robustness as performance *only* on perturbed sequences. This is conceptually sound. However, Table 4 (robustness under consecutive errors) shows F1 (Macro) values like 0.01, 0.02, and F1 (weighted) as low as 0.02–0.03, even while accuracy remains at 6% or above. For multi-class classification, such near-zero macro F1 suggests near-total failure across most classes — yet the accuracy is non-trivial. This discrepancy is suspicious. Macro F1 averages per-class F1 scores, so extremely low values imply the model fails on nearly all rare classes. However, given that the training set is only 1% (Section 4), and many variants are underrepresented, it's plausible that the model collapses on perturbed sequences for rare classes. Still, the lack of explanation for such extreme degradation — especially when overall accuracy remains moderate — raises concern. The issue isn't with the definition but with the plausibility and interpretation: such poor macro F1 with non-zero accuracy suggests possible miscalculation or unreported class imbalance mitigation. Without details on label distribution or handling of rare classes, this remains a questionable but not invalid claim.

comments.title: Mismatch between training data size and model complexity
comments.quote: For the classification algorithms, we use 1% data for training and 99% for testing.
comments.explanation: The dataset has ~2 million sequences, so 1% training means ~20,000 sequences. The k-mer frequency vector has 9261 dimensions, and the Keras classifier uses a fully connected layer with 9261 neurons — implying tens of millions of parameters. Training such a model on only 20k examples risks severe overfitting, especially without regularization (not mentioned). Yet the model achieves 87% accuracy (Table 2), which seems implausibly high for such a high-dimensional, low-sample regime unless the task is extremely easy or data leakage occurs. However, the authors cite Spike2Vec (Ali & Patterson, 2021) as using the same split, suggesting this is a replication choice. While the reproduction intention mitigates concerns about intent, it does not resolve the statistical implausibility. Standard ML wisdom would require regularization, dropout, or simpler architectures — none mentioned. This underspecification of model training details (optimizer settings, convergence behavior, validation) is problematic.

comments.title: Ambiguity in adversarial sequence generation procedure
comments.quote: For each of the spike sequence in the set of errored sequences, we randomly select a fraction of amino acids (i.e. 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%) and flip their value randomly.
comments.explanation: The term 'flip their value randomly' is ambiguous. Does 'flip' mean substitute with a uniformly random amino acid from the 21-letter alphabet, or with a biologically plausible substitution (e.g. BLOSUM-based)? The latter is more realistic, but the paper gives no details. Moreover, flipping 20% of 1273 amino acids means ~255 substitutions per sequence — an extremely high mutation rate compared to biological reality (SARS-CoV-2 accumulates ~33 genomic mutations *per year*). While this is a stress test, such extreme perturbations may render sequences biologically meaningless, making robustness results less interpretable. The method is underspecified: the distribution of substitutions, handling of stop codons, and biological plausibility are all omitted, limiting reproducibility and real-world relevance.

comments.title: Training runtime reported but training not repeated across perturbations
comments.quote: The batch size and number of epochs are taken as 100 and 10, respectively for training the model.
comments.explanation: The authors train the model once on clean training data and evaluate on perturbed test sets. This is standard for robustness evaluation. However, Tables 3–6 report different 'Train. runtime (sec.)' values for different error levels, implying retraining — but the text says nothing about retraining. For instance, Table 3 shows training times from ~11k to ~15k seconds across configurations, but the model is not retrained when only the test set changes. This is a critical inconsistency. The most plausible explanation is that 'Train. runtime' is mislabeled and actually refers to *evaluation* time or total experiment runtime. Otherwise, reporting differing training times for identical training procedures suggests either a serious methodological flaw or a labeling error. Given that evaluation on increasingly corrupted test sets could take variable time (if implemented inefficiently), this might explain variation — but it should be labeled as such.

comments.title: Claim of DL robustness not fully supported by robustness metric results
comments.quote: we show that deep learning is generally more robust in handling these errors than machine learning models.
comments.explanation: The paper supports this claim primarily with results from the Keras classifier (DL) versus traditional ML models (NB, LR, RC) using k-mer inputs. However, across Tables 4 and 6 (robustness on perturbed sequences), performance collapses dramatically — e.g., macro F1 drops to 0.01–0.03 in many cases. While DL may outperform ML in relative terms, both fail catastrophically under certain perturbations. The claim of 'more robust' is technically true if DL > ML, but the absolute performance suggests neither is truly 'robust' in a practical sense. The claim overstates what is shown: the models degrade significantly under perturbation, which contradicts the strong conclusion that DL is robust. A more nuanced statement acknowledging severe degradation but superior relative performance would be warranted.

comments.title: k-mer count formula omitted and inconsistent with context
comments.quote: The total number of k-mers in a given spike sequences are: where N is the length of the sequence.
comments.explanation: The sentence states a formula is coming but provides none — a clear OCR or typesetting failure. The next sentence correctly notes that for k=3 and N=1273, there are 1273−3+1=1271 k-mers per sequence. This is correct for a sliding window. But then the frequency vector is defined as having length 21^3 = 9261 — which is the *vocabulary* size, not the number of k-mers in one sequence. This distinction is crucial: the count vector is over all possible 9261 k-mers, but only ~1271 are present per sequence. The explanation in 3.2.1 correctly describes this. So while the missing formula is a defect, the context resolves it. The intent is clear: they use a fixed-size count vector over the full k-mer space. The issue is minor but reflects poor presentation.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper presents a valuable framework for benchmarking ML robustness on COVID-19 spike sequences, addressing an important need in the field. The authors introduce sensible error models mimicking sequencing technologies and conduct extensive experiments on a large dataset. However, the paper suffers from several technical issues including missing formulas, inconsistent notation, and insufficient methodological details. The claims about k-mers preserving order and DL robustness are somewhat overstated given the actual results. With clearer specifications and more nuanced claims, this would be a stronger contribution.

comments.title: Missing formula for k-mers count
comments.quote: The total number of k-mers in a given spike sequences are:
comments.explanation: The sentence ends with a colon but no formula follows. This appears to be a typesetting error where the formula was omitted. Based on the context and the example given immediately after (1273 - 3 + 1 = 1271), the formula should likely be N - k + 1, where N is sequence length and k is the k-mer length. This omission makes the text incomplete and confusing for readers.

comments.title: Inconsistent RFF notation
comments.quote: RFF tries to approximate the inner product between any pair of transformed points. where z is low dimensional representation.
comments.explanation: The RFF formula introduces φ(a) and φ(b) but then switches to z without establishing the connection. The sentence "where z is low dimensional representation" appears disconnected from the preceding equation. A proper explanation should clarify that z is the RFF mapping of the original data points, and how it relates to φ.

comments.title: Oversimplified claim about k-mers preserving order
comments.quote: One advantage of using k-mers based approach is that it is an "alignment free" method unlike OHE, which requires the sequences to be aligned to the reference genome.
comments.explanation: The paper repeatedly claims that k-mers preserve order better than OHE (e.g., in Section 5: "since k-mers preserve the order of the amino acids"). However, the frequency vector representation described in Section 3.2.1 counts k-mers without preserving their positions. While k-mers capture local order within the window, the global sequence order is lost in the frequency vector. This oversimplification could mislead readers about the actual representational power of the approach.

comments.title: Ambiguous amino acid flipping mechanism
comments.quote: we randomly select a fraction of amino acids (i.e. 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%) and flip their value randomly.
comments.explanation: The paper doesn't specify what "flip their value randomly" means. Are amino acids substituted with other random amino acids from the 20-letter alphabet? Are substitutions equally likely? Are insertions/deletions considered? This ambiguity makes it impossible to reproduce the error generation process exactly. For Illumina error simulation, substitutions should follow known error profiles (e.g., higher rates for certain transitions).

comments.title: Insufficient justification for k=3 choice
comments.quote: In this paper, we take k = 3 (decided empirically).
comments.explanation: The choice of k=3 for k-mers is stated as "decided empirically" but no empirical evidence or ablation study is provided. Given that k is a critical hyperparameter affecting both representation quality and dimensionality (21^k), readers need to see how performance varies with different k values or at least the rationale for this specific choice.

comments.title: Overstated DL robustness claims
comments.quote: we show that deep learning is generally more robust in handling these errors than machine learning models.
comments.explanation: The robustness results in Tables 4 and 6 show very low scores for keras classifier under many error conditions (e.g., accuracy as low as 0.06 in Table 4). The claim of "generally more robust" appears overstated when robustness scores are often below 0.5. The paper should qualify this claim or provide additional analysis (e.g., statistical tests) to support it.

comments.title: Under-specified deep learning architecture
comments.quote: It contains a fully connected network with one hidden layer that contains neurons equals to the length of the feature vector (i.e. 9261).
comments.explanation: The deep learning model lacks important architectural details. No mention of regularization (dropout, L2), initialization method, or why such a large hidden layer (9261 neurons) was chosen. For a paper focusing on robustness, these architectural choices could significantly impact results and should be justified.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents a framework for benchmarking machine learning and deep learning models against simulated sequencing errors on SARS-CoV-2 spike protein sequences. While the empirical findings are interesting and the attempt to address robustness is well-motivated, the paper suffers from inconsistent mathematical notation, potentially confusing definitions of model input sizes, and a lack of clarity regarding the specific architecture of the 'Keras classifier'. These issues, combined with significant discrepancies between the text and the presented tables, make the reproduction of the methodology challenging.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in k-mer total count
comments.quote:
The total number of k-mers in a given spike sequences are: 


where N is the length of the sequence. The variable k is the user defined parameter. In this paper, we take k = 3 (decided empirically). Since we have 1273 length spike sequences, the total number of k-mers that we can have for any spike sequence is 1273 - 3 + 1 = 1271.
comments.explanation: The text states the total count is 1271, but the formula preceding it is missing from the OCR output. Even assuming the standard formula (N - k + 1), the text later states that the length of the spike sequence is 1273, with a stopping character at 1274. If the sequence length is 1273, the calculation is 1273 - 3 + 1 = 1271. However, the vector length calculation in Section 3.2.1 is 21^3 = 9261. While 9261 is the vocabulary size, it is unclear if the authors are using a simple bag-of-words (counts) or a frequency vector that accounts for sequence length (normalized). The discrepancy between using a fixed-length vector of 9261 and the varying number of k-mers per sequence (if they were to normalize) is ambiguous.

comments.title: Ambiguous input size for hidden layer
comments.quote: It contains a fully connected network with one hidden layer that contains neurons equals to the length of the feature vector (i.e. 9261).
comments.explanation: The authors define the hidden layer size to be equal to the length of the feature vector (9261). In Section 3.1, they state the OHE vector has a length of 26733 (21 * 1273). If the architecture uses a single hidden layer of size 9261, it is mathematically impossible for the network to take the OHE vector of size 26733 as input without a projection or compression layer. The authors do not mention an input projection layer or dimensionality reduction for the OHE case. This is a significant omission for reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistency in dataset size reporting
comments.quote: After this preprocessing, we are left with 1 , 995 , 195 spike sequences.
comments.explanation: The authors define '1,995,195' as the total number after filtering. However, summing the numbers in the 'Num. of sequences' column of Table 1 yields 1,995,195. Yet, the abstract claims 'millions of sequences'. While 1.99M is technically 'nearly two million', claiming 'millions' usually implies >2M. This is a minor framing issue throughout the text, but the precision in Table 1 vs the descriptive text is confusing.

comments.title: Inconsistent terminology for model components
comments.quote: We use ”rectifier” activation function for this classifier. Moreover, we use “softmax” activation function in the output layer.
comments.explanation: The term 'rectifier' usually refers to ReLU. While it is likely the authors mean ReLU, the lack of standard nomenclature (ReLU) is slightly confusing. More importantly, using a softmax layer with 'sparse categorical crossentropy' is correct for multi-class classification, but the paper never explicitly states how many output neurons are used (it should be 1327, the number of variants mentioned). This is an underspecified method detail.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper addresses a relevant and timely need for benchmarking ML/DL robustness on SARS-CoV-2 spike sequences under simulated sequencing errors. The motivation is well-articulated, and the experimental design covers multiple perturbation types and model architectures. However, the manuscript contains several technical and logical issues that impede clarity and reproducibility. Key mathematical formulas are missing, critical descriptions of the adversarial perturbation procedure are phrased backwards, and terminology around evaluation metrics and runtime is inconsistent with standard conventions. Addressing these points will significantly improve the paper's rigor and readability.

comments.title: Missing mathematical formulas in feature extraction sections
comments.quote:
The total number of k-mers in a given spike sequences are: 

 where _N_ is the length of the sequence.
comments.explanation: The text references an equation that is entirely absent, likely dropped during OCR or formatting. Based on the subsequent calculation ($1273 - 3 + 1 = 1271$), the intended formula is $N - k + 1$. A similar omission occurs in Section 3.2.1 where the formal mapping for Random Fourier Features (RFF) is referenced but the equation is missing. These gaps hinder reproducibility, as readers cannot verify the exact mathematical transformations used for dimensionality reduction and feature generation.

comments.title: Backwards description of adversarial sequence replacement
comments.quote: At the end, we replace these errored sequences set with the corresponding set of original spike sequences in the test set.
comments.explanation: This sentence literally states that the perturbed (errored) sequences are replaced by the original clean sequences. This is the opposite of the intended experimental procedure. The authors likely meant to write 'replace the corresponding original spike sequences with these errored sequences.' As written, this ambiguity could mislead readers about how the adversarial test sets are actually constructed and populated.

comments.title: Incorrect claim regarding OHE and sequence order preservation
comments.quote: If we compute the pair-wise euclidean distance between any two OHE based vectors, the overall distance will not be effected if a random pair of amino acids are swapped for those two feature vectors.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly establish that position-aware one-hot encoding uses a vector of length $L \times |\Sigma|$, which inherently preserves order by assigning distinct dimensions to each position. However, they subsequently claim that swapping amino acids does not affect the Euclidean distance, which is false for position-aware OHE (swapping positions changes the one-hot vectors at those indices, altering the distance). The reasoning conflates position-aware OHE with a bag-of-words or unanchored encoding. This technical inaccuracy undermines the motivation for using OHE as a baseline and needs correction.

comments.title: Mislabeled 'Train. runtime' column in robustness tables
comments.quote:
Train.
runtime
(sec.)|
comments.explanation: Tables 3 and 4 report performance metrics across different perturbation settings applied to the test set. The models are not retrained for each setting; only inference is evaluated. Therefore, the column labeled 'Train. runtime' actually reflects inference or evaluation time. This mislabeling creates confusion regarding the computational costs and could lead readers to incorrectly assess the training overhead of the proposed pipeline.

comments.title: Non-standard and ambiguous metric naming
comments.quote: One strategy is called Accuracy and the other is called robustness. In the case of the Accuracy, we compute the average accuracy, precision, recall, F1 (weighted), F1 (Macro), and ROC-AUC for the whole test set including adversarial and non-adversarial examples.
comments.explanation: Labeling performance on a mixed clean/perturbed dataset as 'Accuracy' and performance on the perturbed subset as 'Robustness' deviates from standard terminology. Typically, 'accuracy' denotes clean-data performance, while robustness measures describe degradation or performance under attack. The current naming obscures the clean-data baseline in the robustness tables and could cause readers to misinterpret which values correspond to unperturbed performance versus perturbed performance.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper tackles an important problem—benchmarking ML robustness for SARS-CoV-2 spike sequence classification—and provides interesting comparisons between OHE and k-mer embeddings, as well as between ML and DL methods. However, several technical concerns undermine the validity of the claims. The most critical is that the 'consecutive error' generation method does not actually simulate indels (it only flips amino acids), yet the paper explicitly claims it models PacBio indel errors. Additionally, the authors state they use aligned spike sequences while touting k-mers as alignment-free, creating a methodological contradiction. The DL architecture with a hidden layer of 9261 neurons is extremely large and raises overfitting concerns without discussion of regularization. The random error generation is also underspecified regarding whether the flipped amino acid is guaranteed to differ from the original. These issues should be addressed before the paper can be considered reliable.

comments.title: Consecutive error generation does not model indels
comments.quote: Consecutive errors, on the other hand, are small subsequences of consective errors, which can model insertion-delection (indel) errors which are common in third generation long-reads technologies such as Pacific Biosciences (PacBio) SMRT sequencing (Dohm et al., 2020).
comments.explanation: The paper claims that flipping a consecutive block of amino acids models indel errors. However, indels change the length of the sequence and shift the reading frame; simply substituting consecutive residues does not reproduce the effect of an insertion or deletion. This is a fundamental mismatch between the error simulation and the claimed real-world error profile. The method introduces substitution errors, not indels. Therefore, the benchmarking does not actually test robustness to PacBio-like errors as stated, and the conclusions about robustness to such errors are unsubstantiated.

comments.title: Inconsistency between aligned sequences and alignment-free claim
comments.quote: We used the (aligned) spike protein from a popular and publicly available database of SARS-CoV-2 sequences, GISAID.
comments.explanation: The paper explicitly states that the spike sequences used are aligned, yet it claims that the k-mer approach is alignment-free. If the sequences have been pre-aligned (e.g., to a reference), then the k-mer frequencies are computed from those aligned positions, which may introduce biases and artificial conservation of length. The k-mer method is indeed alignment-free in principle, but applying it to already-aligned sequences means the comparison is not a clean test of alignment-free versus alignment-dependent methods. This contradiction weakens the evaluation of the k-mer embedding's claimed advantage.

comments.title: Underspecified random error generation
comments.quote: we randomly select a fraction of amino acids (i.e. 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%) and flip their value randomly
comments.explanation: The phrase 'flip their value randomly' is ambiguous: it is unclear whether the new value is chosen uniformly from the set of all 20 standard amino acids (including possibly the same one) or from the other 19. If the same amino acid can be chosen, then no error occurs in that position, making the effective error rate lower than intended. This ambiguity affects the reproducibility of the adversarial attack and the interpretation of robustness results. The authors should specify the exact procedure for selecting the replacement amino acid.

comments.title: Overly large neural network without regularization discussion
comments.quote: It contains a fully connected network with one hidden layer that contains neurons equals to the length of the feature vector (i.e. 9261).
comments.explanation: A hidden layer with 9261 neurons leads to approximately 85 million parameters (9261*9261 + biases), which is extremely large relative to the training set size (~20k sequences). This design is prone to overfitting, yet the paper does not mention any regularization (dropout, weight decay, early stopping) or validation strategy to mitigate this. While the reported test accuracy is high, the lack of discussion on overfitting and generalization is a gap, especially for a benchmarking paper that aims to assess robustness.

comments.title: Selective reporting of robustness results
comments.quote: To visually compare the accuracy of the average accuracy for the consecutive errored sequences approach, we plot the average accuracies in Figure 4a. Similarly, Figure 5 (in Appendix) contains the robustness results for the same two approaches.
comments.explanation: In the main text, only the Keras classifier with k-mers is presented for adversarial robustness results (Tables 3 and 4). The other ML methods (Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression, Ridge) on adversarial data are relegated to the appendix. While this is not incorrect, the paper claims to benchmark 'a wide array of ML approaches', yet the central robustness comparison in the main body is limited to one method. A fair benchmarking would include all methods in the main comparison to support claims about DL vs ML robustness. This selective presentation may overstate the superiority of the DL approach.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a timely and relevant benchmarking framework for assessing the robustness of machine learning and deep learning models against realistic sequencing errors in SARS-CoV-2 spike sequence classification. The methodology is sound, utilizing established approaches like one-hot encoding (OHE), k-mer frequency vectors, and deep neural networks, and compares them effectively against classical baselines. While the main conclusions are valid—that deep learning and k-mer representations offer robustness advantages over OHE and traditional ML—the paper contains a few minor issues. These include an OCR-induced notation error in the frequency vector calculation, a flawed mathematical justification for why OHE fails to preserve order, and a lack of specification regarding rounding procedures in the adversarial attack generation. These do not significantly undermine the study's findings but should be addressed for clarity.

comments.title: Notation Inconsistency in k-mer Vector Calculation
comments.quote: Now, length of the frequency vector will be $|\Sigma|[k]$ (all possible combinations of k-mers in $\Sigma$ of length $k$). Recall that in our dataset, we have 21 unique amino acids in any spike sequence. Therefore, the length of frequency vector in our case would be $21[3] = 9261$ (when we take $k = 3$).
comments.explanation: The notation used in the mathematical expression, $|\Sigma|[k]$, appears to be an OCR error or typographical mistake. The subsequent sentence uses the same notation with an equals sign, $21[3] = 9261$, to arrive at the number 9261. The number 9261 is the result of $21^3$ ($21 	imes 21 	imes 21$), which represents the total number of all possible 3-mers (k-mers) from a 21-letter alphabet. If $[k]$ is interpreted literally (e.g., as a floor or bracket notation), the calculation $21[3]$ does not yield 9261. While the context clearly resolves the intended meaning (exponentiation), the notation itself is mathematically incorrect and could confuse a reader.

comments.title: Flawed Explanation for OHE Permutation Invariance
comments.quote: If we compute the pair-wise euclidean distance between any two OHE based vectors, the overall distance will not be effected if a random pair of amino acids are swapped for those two feature vectors.
comments.explanation: The reasoning provided to support the claim that OHE fails to preserve the order of amino acids is mathematically questionable. The text suggests that swapping a random pair of amino acids in one feature vector relative to another does not change their Euclidean distance. In standard one-hot encoding (a flattened vector), swapping amino acids at different positions changes the indices of the '1's in the vector. Consequently, the Euclidean distance between the two vectors will typically change (unless they are identical). The authors likely intend to argue that OHE is 'permutation invariant' in the sense that it does not capture the order of sequences (e.g., [ABC] and [BAC] produce different vectors than [ABC] and [ABC]), but the specific explanation involving the distance not being 'effected' by swapping is incorrect.

comments.title: Underspecified Rounding in Adversarial Attack Generation
comments.quote: For each of the spike sequence in the set of errored sequences, we randomly select a fraction of amino acids (i.e. 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%) and flip their value randomly.
comments.explanation: To implement the adversarial attacks, the authors apply a percentage (e.g., 20%) of the amino acid sequence length (1273) to be mutated. However, the paper does not specify how to handle the resulting non-integer number of amino acids that must be flipped (e.g., $0.20 	imes 1273 = 254.6$). Specifically, one must decide whether to floor, round, or ceil this value. While this is a standard convention in programming, its omission creates an ambiguity in the exact implementation of the robustness benchmark, which is a core contribution of the paper.
